title: Volatility vs. permanent loss of capital
subtitle: These are not the same thing. Confusing them is one of the most expensive mistakes an investor can make.

body.content: A stock that drops 30% in a year is scary. It might also be completely fine. The price fell — but if the business is intact, the drop is temporary. This is volatility. Permanent loss of capital is different: the business fails, goes to zero, or you sell at the bottom and never recover. Confusing these two things causes investors to do exactly the wrong thing at exactly the wrong moment.
The S&P 500's history of terrifying recoveries

The S&P 500 has declined more than 20% nine separate times since 1950. Every single time, it has eventually recovered and reached new highs. The 2008 financial crisis: the index dropped 57% from peak to trough. By 2013, it was back at highs. COVID-2020: dropped 34% in five weeks. Recovered entirely within six months. The 2022 bear market: down 25%. Recovery followed. If you held through each of these, you were fine. If you sold at the bottom, you turned a temporary loss into a permanent one.

body.label: The math
body.content: Here's the asymmetry that trips people up: a 50% loss requires a 100% gain to break even. If your portfolio drops from $100,000 to $50,000, you need it to double just to get back to where you started. This is why not selling at the bottom is so important. Paper losses are symmetric. Realized losses are not.

A paper loss is the drop in value of an investment you still hold. It exists on paper — or on your screen — but it hasn't happened yet in any meaningful financial sense. A realized loss is when you sell. The moment you sell below your cost, the loss is permanent. Your portfolio can't recover from a realized loss. Markets can recover from volatility. They can't recover from you panic-selling.
When does volatility become real danger?

items: When your time horizon is wrong: money you need in 18 months shouldn't be in equities
When you're forced to sell: job loss, emergency, or margin call can force selling at the worst time
When you can't stomach it and panic: emotional selling converts paper losses into realized losses
When the underlying business genuinely fails: Enron went to zero — that's permanent loss, not volatility

body.content: The financial media covers market drops the same way it covers natural disasters. Breathless. Urgent. Implying action is required. It isn't. For a long-term investor, the correct response to a 30% market drop is usually: check that your allocation still makes sense, make sure you're not forced to sell, and then do nothing. This is genuinely hard for humans wired to respond to threats. But 'do nothing' is usually the right trade.

body.label: Real talk
body.content: In 2020, between February and late March, the S&P 500 dropped 34% in 33 days. Investors who sold in panic locked in a 34% loss. Investors who bought more got one of the best entry points in a decade. Investors who did nothing recovered fully within six months. The market doesn't care about your feelings. It cares about your decisions.

The actual risk for most long-term investors isn't volatility. It's reacting to volatility. Build a portfolio you can hold through a 40% drawdown without panic-selling. If you can't imagine doing that with your current allocation, you're taking on too much risk — not because the returns will be bad, but because your behavior will be.

Volatility is temporary. Selling at the bottom makes it permanent. A 50% loss needs a 100% gain to recover — which is why not realizing the loss matters so much. For long-term investors, the danger isn't the market dropping. It's reacting to the drop.
